quiz.title: Unit 5 Quiz 9, Grade 11 Chemistry
quiz.subtitle: Transition Metal Compound Formulas
quiz.subject: Chemistry
quiz.topic: Writing Formulas for Ionic Compounds with Transition Metals, Variable Oxidation States, Stock System

questions.question: What is the formula for Lead(II) bromide?
questions.explanation: Lead(II) bromide is $\text{PbBr}_2$. Lead(II) has a +2 charge ($\text{Pb}^{2+}$), and bromide is $\text{Br}^-$ with a -1 charge, so two bromides are needed.

questions.question: What is the formula for Tin(IV) oxide?
questions.explanation: Tin(IV) oxide is $\text{SnO}_2$. Tin(IV) has a +4 charge ($\text{Sn}^{4+}$), and oxide is $\text{O}^{2-}$ with a -2 charge, so two oxides are needed to balance the +4 charge.

questions.question: What is the formula for Mercury(I) chloride?
questions.explanation: Mercury(I) chloride is $\text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2$. Mercury(I) exists as the diatomic ion $\text{Hg}_2^{2+}$ (two mercury atoms sharing a +2 charge). Each chloride is $\text{Cl}^-$, so the formula is $\text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2$, often called calomel.

questions.question: What is the formula for Nickel(II) fluoride?
questions.explanation: Nickel(II) fluoride is $\text{NiF}_2$. Nickel(II) has a +2 charge ($\text{Ni}^{2+}$), and fluoride is $\text{F}^-$ with a -1 charge, so two fluorides are needed.

questions.question: What is the formula for Cobalt(III) oxide?
questions.explanation: Cobalt(III) oxide is $\text{Co}_2\text{O}_3$. Cobalt(III) has a +3 charge ($\text{Co}^{3+}$), and oxide is $\text{O}^{2-}$. To balance charges, two $\text{Co}^{3+}$ (+6) combine with three $\text{O}^{2-}$ (-6).

questions.question: What is the formula for Zinc sulfide?
questions.explanation: Zinc sulfide is $\text{ZnS}$. Zinc typically forms $\text{Zn}^{2+}$, and sulfide is $\text{S}^{2-}$, so they combine in a 1:1 ratio. Zinc has only one common oxidation state (+2), so no Roman numeral is needed in the name.

questions.question: What is the formula for Mercury(I) bromide?
questions.explanation: Mercury(I) bromide is $\text{Hg}_2\text{Br}_2$. Mercury(I) exists as the diatomic ion $\text{Hg}_2^{2+}$ (two mercury atoms sharing a +2 charge). Each bromide is $\text{Br}^-$ with a -1 charge, so two bromides are needed to balance the +2 charge. The formula is often written as $\text{Hg}_2\text{Br}_2$.

questions.question: What is the formula for Mercury(I) iodide?
questions.explanation: Mercury(I) iodide is $\text{Hg}_2\text{I}_2$. Mercury(I) exists as the diatomic ion $\text{Hg}_2^{2+}$ (two mercury atoms sharing a +2 charge). Each iodide is $\text{I}^-$ with a -1 charge, so two iodides are needed to balance the +2 charge. The formula is $\text{Hg}_2\text{I}_2$, analogous to mercury(I) chloride (calomel).

questions.question: What is the formula for Nickel(II) oxide?
questions.explanation: Nickel(II) oxide is $\text{NiO}$. Nickel(II) has a +2 charge ($\text{Ni}^{2+}$), and oxide is $\text{O}^{2-}$, so they combine in a 1:1 ratio.

questions.question: What is the formula for Chromium(III) oxide?
questions.explanation: Chromium(III) oxide is $\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3$. Chromium(III) has a +3 charge ($\text{Cr}^{3+}$), and oxide is $\text{O}^{2-}$. To balance charges, two $\text{Cr}^{3+}$ (+6) combine with three $\text{O}^{2-}$ (-6).

questions.question: What is the formula for Silver(I) chloride?
questions.explanation: Silver(I) chloride is $\text{AgCl}$. Silver(I) has a +1 charge ($\text{Ag}^+$), and chloride is $\text{Cl}^-$, so they combine in a 1:1 ratio. Silver does not commonly form other charges, so the (I) is often omitted.

questions.question: What is the formula for Gold(III) chloride?
questions.explanation: Gold(III) chloride is $\text{AuCl}_3$. Gold(III) has a +3 charge ($\text{Au}^{3+}$), and chloride is $\text{Cl}^-$ with a -1 charge, so three chlorides are needed.

questions.question: What is the formula for Gold(I) chloride?
questions.explanation: Gold(I) chloride is $\text{AuCl}$. Gold(I) has a +1 charge ($\text{Au}^+$), and chloride is $\text{Cl}^-$, so they combine in a 1:1 ratio. Gold(III) chloride (D) is $\text{AuCl}_3$.

questions.question: What is the formula for Copper(I) bromide?
questions.explanation: Copper(I) bromide is $\text{CuBr}$. Copper(I) has a +1 charge ($\text{Cu}^+$), and bromide is $\text{Br}^-$, so they combine in a 1:1 ratio. $\text{CuBr}_2$ (B) would be copper(II) bromide.

questions.question: What is the formula for Copper(II) bromide?
questions.explanation: Copper(II) bromide is $\text{CuBr}_2$. Copper(II) has a +2 charge ($\text{Cu}^{2+}$), and bromide is $\text{Br}^-$, so two bromides are needed. $\text{CuBr}$ (A) would be copper(I) bromide.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered writing formulas for transition metal compounds! 🧪📝
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review the special case of mercury(I) and common oxidation states.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on balancing charges for metals with higher oxidation states.
scoreMessages.0: Please review how to determine the charge of transition metals and balance with anions.
